title: The First Hour
description: The first hour is where discipline separates execution from impulse.

blocks.content: The gun goes off. A gap opens. Someone surges. Your legs want to follow — you're fresh, amped, and the chimp says GO. Every molecule of adrenaline is screaming one word: CHASE. So you chase. You feel incredible. Powerful. Invincible. For about 15 minutes.

Then you start paying for it. The pace that felt effortless was actually threshold. The gap you closed cost you a match you'll desperately need four hours from now. The cruelest part: it felt RIGHT. It didn't feel like a mistake until it was too late to fix.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a pre-written First-Hour Focus Script.

blocks.question: The Pre-Race Protocol: Minutes 1-5 = ___, Minutes 6-8 = ___.
blocks.explanation: Correct. The protocol sequence: 6-2-7 breathing first (physiological foundation), then Highlight Reel visualization (builds on the calm).

blocks.content: The first hour is where discipline separates execution from impulse. Adrenaline makes hard paces feel easy — your brain's cost-prediction is lagging behind the expenditure. This is not the time to feel your way through. It's the time to execute a plan.

items.title: Attentional Focus — Broad-External and Narrow-Internal
items.content: Alternate between broad-external (positioning, terrain, group energy) and narrow-internal (breathing rate, leg sensation, RPE). Don't get stuck in either. When the chimp locks your attention on a gap or competitor, redirect: 'Spotlight — come back. What's my RPE?' This is the Spotlight Drill under fire.

items.title: Adrenaline Override — Why 'Easy' Feels Easy
items.content: Perceived effort in early exercise reflects brain motor command output, not peripheral fatigue (De Morree, Klein, & Marcora, 2012). Going out too hard FEELS EASY. In training, the chimp adds 1-3 RPE points. In the first hour of a race, adrenaline SUBTRACTS 1-3 points. A 7 feels like a 5. Threshold feels like tempo.

items.title: RPE First, Data for Confirmation
items.content: RPE increases linearly with proportion of race completed regardless of actual conditions (Faulkner, Parfitt, & Eston, 2008). In the first hour, if your RPE is low and your data says you're at or above plan — you're going too hard. The adrenaline discount is active. Trust data early, trust RPE later.

items.label: Mantra: "Settle. Execute. The race is long."
items.detail: When the gun goes off and the chimp screams GO, answer with this. When a gap opens and your legs want to chase, answer with this. Instructional self-talk — three specific commands: settle your effort, execute your plan, remember the duration.

items.label: Check Pacing Every 10-15 Minutes
items.detail: Run a quick diagnostic. Two questions: 'What's my RPE?' and 'What does my data say?' Compare them. In the first hour, if RPE is low and data says at-or-above plan, you're going too hard. The adrenaline discount is active. Back off.

items.label: Deploy Performance Statements
items.detail: When anxiety spikes — 'You're going too slow,' 'They're all faster,' 'You don't belong here' — fire your pain-response Performance Statement. The chimp produces negative chess pieces. Your Performance Statements answer them with action-directed positive thought.

items.label: Resist Every Urge to Chase
items.detail: Your If-Then Plans fire here: 'If someone surges in the first 10 minutes, I will hold my pace and let them go.' 'If my heart rate is above zone 2 in the first 20 minutes, I will back off regardless of how it feels.' The decision was already made — last week, with a clear head.

blocks.prompt: Mile 3 of a 100-mile gravel race. A group of 5 riders surges 15 seconds ahead. Your legs are fresh. The chimp is screaming: don't let them go. Your race plan says hold pace for the first hour.

options.label: A
options.result: The chimp wins. At mile 3, you have no idea if this group will hold. You've abandoned your race plan based on a 15-second gap and fresh legs. By mile 30, the effort tax from that early surge compounds. By mile 60, you're alone and paying interest on a loan you took out at mile 3.

options.label: B
options.result: The coach wins. You've demonstrated exactly the discipline the first hour demands. The mantra works: 'Settle. Execute. The race is long.' At mile 60, when those riders are fading, you'll be passing them with a controlled effort and a clear head.

options.label: C
options.result: The worst of both worlds. You've violated your race plan AND failed to catch the group. You're now in no-man's land — faster than planned, slower than needed, and burning matches with no return. The half-commitment costs more than either full commitment.

blocks.content: The race isn't won in the first hour. But it can be lost there.
Next — the moment where the race IS decided.
